coverage_results.query: Search the web for the history of Castelo de São Jorge.
coverage_results.response:
I detected English — here is a concise web-based history of Castelo de São Jorge.

- Human occupation of the castle hill dates back to at least the 8th century BC.  
- The oldest fortifications on the site originate around the 2nd century BC, and the hill was successively fortified/occupied by Phoenicians, Carthaginians, Romans and Moors.  
- Under Muslim rule the site formed part of the defensive system of al-Ushbuna (Lisbon).  
- In 1147 the hill and its fortress were captured by Afonso I of Portugal (with help from Northern European crusaders) during the Siege of Lisbon; the castle then became a royal palace.  
- Over the medieval and early modern periods the castle served multiple roles: royal residence, military barracks and, at one point, housing the Torre do Tombo (the national archives).  
- The 1755 Lisbon earthquake caused substantial damage to the castle; later restoration and archaeological work in the 20th century recovered and conserved many structures.  
- Today Castelo de São Jorge is a national monument and museum offering archaeological remains, restored battlements and panoramic views over Lisbon.

📌 Source: [*Wikipedia – São Jorge Castle*](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Jorge_Castle)
coverage_results.retrieved_context:
[search_history_culture] returned:
📚 **Wikipédia: São Jorge Castle**
🔗 URL: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Jorge_Castle

São Jorge Castle (Portuguese: Castelo de São Jorge; Portuguese pronunciation: [kɐʃˈtɛlu dɨ sɐ̃w̃ ˈʒɔɾʒɨ]), sometimes known in English as Saint George's Castle, is a historic castle in the Portuguese capital of Lisbon, located in the freguesia of Santa Maria Maior. Human occupation of the castle hill dates to at least the 8th century BC while the oldest fortifications on the site date from the 2nd century BC. The hill on which Saint George's Castle stands has played an important part in the history of Lisbon, having served as the location of fortifications occupied successively by Phoenicians, Carthaginians, Romans, and Moors, before its conquest by the Portuguese in the 1147 Siege of Lisbon. Since the 12th century, the castle has variously served as a royal palace, a military barracks, home of the Torre do Tombo National Archive, and now as a national monument and museum.
